result.markdown:
# Quarto and R {#sec-r}

When you include R code cells in your document, Quarto uses the knitr engine to execute them.
The basics of code cells, including syntax and common options like `echo` and `include`, are covered in @sec-code.
This chapter covers the features specific to R and knitr.

## Output {#sec-r-output}

### Inline code {#sec-inline-code}

In addition to code cells, you can embed R code directly within your markdown text using inline code, inserting computed values into your narrative without manually copying them.

The syntax uses single backticks with `{r}` immediately after the opening backtick:

```markdown
The mean temperature was `{{r}} mean(airquality$Temp)` degrees.
```

We recommend that you avoid complex function calls or operations that do significant work in inline code and instead compute values in a code cell and then reference them inline:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
avg_temp <- mean(airquality$Temp)
```
:::


```markdown
The mean temperature was `{{r}} avg_temp` degrees.
```

This renders as: The mean temperature was 77.8823529 degrees.

By default, inline code produces plain text.
To generate markdown-formatted output, wrap the result in `I()`:

```markdown
The result is `{{r}} I("**significant**")`.
```

This produces bold text in the rendered output.

::: callout-note
## Inline code in YAML

Inline code cannot be used directly in YAML strings, such as titles or figure captions.
For dynamic captions, use the cross-reference div syntax instead (see @sec-crossreferences).
:::

### Data frame printing {#sec-df-print}

Quarto provides the `df-print` option to control how data frames appear in your output, and it can be set at the document-level in the YAML header:

```yaml
---
title: "My Analysis"
format:
  html:
    df-print: paged
---
```

The `default` option uses the standard S3 print method, i.e., tables appear as they would in the R console.
The `tibble` option prints the table as plain text tibble, which is equivalent to wrapping the resulting table with `tibble::tibble()`.
The `kable` option applies `knitr::kable()` and supports various output formats, including HTML, PDF, and Word.
In HTML documents, the `paged` option enables interactive tables with pagination.

### Graphics devices {#sec-graphics-devices}

When R generates a plot, knitr uses a *graphics device* to save it to a file, which is then included in the output.
The default device is `png`, and it works well in most situations.
You may want to change it to improve text rendering, achieve scalable output, or ensure PDF compatibility.

You can set the graphics device with the `dev` option, either at the document level or for individual cells:

```yaml
---
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: ragg_png
---
```

Or for a single cell:

```{{r}}
#| dev: svglite
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point()
```

Common choices include the following:

- The `png` device is the default. It creates raster images that are fast to generate.
- The `ragg_png` device creates higher-quality PNG images for better text rendering and anti-aliasing. Generating images figures with this device requires the [**ragg**](https://ragg.r-lib.org/) package.
- The `svglite` device creates scalable HTML output in vector format. Figures created with this device are sharp at any zoom level and they require the [**svglite**](https://svglite.r-lib.org/) package.
- The `pdf` device generates figures in standard PDF format. It is the default vector device for LaTeX/PDF output.
- The `cairo_pdf` device generates figures in PDF format with Unicode text. It is especially useful for handling non-Latin characters and special symbols.

::: callout-tip
## Automatic device selection for PDF

The R option `knitr.graphics.auto_pdf` (shown in @sec-execution-options-r) tells knitr to automatically use a PDF graphics device when rendering to PDF output and a PNG device otherwise.
That way, you don't need to set `dev` differently for each format.
:::

While not knitr-specific, the `fig-format` option is also worth knowing about alongside `dev`.
It works across all Quarto engines:

```yaml
---
format:
  html:
    fig-format: svg
---
```

Use this when you only need to change the output file format without customizing the specific graphics device.
When both `dev` and `fig-format` are set, `dev` takes precedence.

### Multiple figures from a code cell {#sec-multiple-figures}

A single R code cell can produce multiple plots.
By default, each plot appears as a separate figure in the output.
Use `fig-cap` and `fig-subcap` to arrange them as labeled subfigures.

You can provide `fig-cap` as the overall caption and `fig-subcap` as a list of individual subcaptions:

```{{r}}
#| label: fig-mtcars-panels
#| fig-cap: "Motor Trend car data"
#| fig-subcap:
#|   - "Weight vs. fuel efficiency"
#|   - "Displacement vs. fuel efficiency"
#| layout-ncol: 2
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point()

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = disp, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point()
```

This produces two side-by-side subfigures, each with its own caption, under a shared parent caption.
The individual panels are cross-referenceable as `@fig-mtcars-panels-1` and `@fig-mtcars-panels-2`, while `@fig-mtcars-panels` refers to the group.

Additionally, the `layout-ncol` option controls how many columns the subfigures occupy.
For more complex arrangements, you can use the `layout` option to specify custom grids:

```{{r}}
#| label: fig-custom-layout
#| fig-cap: A figure with custom layout
#| fig-subcap:
#|   - Plot A
#|   - Plot B
#|   - Plot C
#| layout: [[1,1], [1]]
plot(1:10)
plot(10:1)
plot(1:20)
```

The `layout: [[1,1], [1]]` specification places the first two plots side by side in the first row, with the third plot spanning the full width of the second row.
For a full explanation of the layout syntax, see @sec-layout.

### Interactive output {#sec-interactive-output-r}

Many R packages produce interactive HTML output---plots, tables, maps---through the [htmlwidgets](https://www.htmlwidgets.org/) framework.
Load the package and call the function as you would in an interactive R session.
For example, you can use code cells that produce interactive plots with [plotly](https://plotly.com/r/):

```{{r}}
#| eval: false
p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point()

ggplotly(p)
```

Or interactive tables with [DT](https://rstudio.github.io/DT/):

```{{r}}
#| eval: false
DT::datatable(mtcars, options = list(pageLength = 5))
```

Or interactive maps with [leaflet](https://rstudio.github.io/leaflet/):

```{{r}}
#| eval: false
leaflet() |>
  addTiles() |>
  addMarkers(lng = -73.99, lat = 40.73, popup = "New York City")
```

It is important to note that interactive widgets only work in HTML output formats.
When rendering to PDF or Word, widgets will either produce a static fallback image or an error, depending on the package.
You can use `knitr::is_html_output()` (see @sec-conditional-execution) to conditionally include widgets only in HTML output.

::: callout-note
## Widget dependencies and self-contained documents

Each HTML widget brings its own JavaScript and CSS dependencies.
In self-contained HTML documents (`embed-resources: true`), these dependencies are bundled into the file, which can increase its size.
If your document uses many different widget packages, consider using the default non-self-contained output and hosting the result as a directory.
:::

## Configuration {#sec-r-configuration}

### Execution options {#sec-execution-options-r}

@sec-code introduced cell-level options like `echo` and `include`.
Many of these options can also be set at the document level to apply to all code cells.

#### Document-level options

Set execution options for the entire document using the `execute` key in your YAML header:

```yaml
---
title: "My Report"
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---
```

Common document-level execution options include the following and take on values `true` or `false`, unless specified otherwise:

- `eval`: Whether to execute code cells
- `echo`: Whether to display source code
- `output`: Whether to include results (can also be set to `asis`)
- `warning`: Whether to include warnings
- `error`: Whether to include errors (without halting)
- `include`: Whether to include any output at all

Additionally, `fig-width` and `fig-height` can be set at the document level to define default figure dimensions in inches.

#### Knitr-specific options

The knitr engine has additional options that you can set using the `knitr` key.
These map directly to knitr's chunk options:

```yaml
---
title: "My Document"
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    collapse: true
    comment: "#>"
    R.options:
      knitr.graphics.auto_pdf: true
---
```

`R.options` temporarily sets R options via `options()` before each code cell executes.
The original values are restored afterward.

#### Cell-level options

Individual code cells can override document-level settings.
The knitr engine supports a large set of cell-level options.

- Some options control code appearance:
  - `code-fold`: Collapse code into an expandable section
  - `code-summary`: Text shown for collapsed code (e.g., "Show code")
  - `code-line-numbers`: Add line numbers to code

- Some control the output:
  - `results`: How to display text results (`markup`, `asis`, `hold`, `hide`)
  - `collapse`: Merge code and output into a single block
  - `comment`: Prefix for output lines (default `"##"`)

- Some are specific to code cells that generate figures:
  - `fig-cap`: Caption
  - `fig-alt`: Alternative text
  - `fig-align`: Alignment: `left`, `center`, or `right`
  - `fig-dpi`: Resolution in dots per inch
  - `fig-width` and `fig-height`: Figure dimensions in inches
  - `out-width` and `out-height`: Output dimensions as percentage (80%), pixels (400px), or page metrics (0.5\linewidth)

- Finally, some relate to caching:
  - `cache`: Enable caching for the cell
  - `dependson`: Specify cells this one depends on

For a complete list of knitr cell options, see the [Quarto reference](https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-knitr.html).

#### Raw markdown output

Sometimes you want your R code to produce raw markdown that Quarto processes directly, rather than being wrapped in output containers.
Use `output: asis` for this:

```{{r}}
#| output: asis
cat("## A Dynamic Heading\n\n")
cat("This paragraph was generated by R.\n")
```

One common use case is programmatically generating sections in a loop.

### Parameters {#sec-parameters}

Parameters let you create variations of a document without editing the source---for example, generating the same report for different regions, time periods, or scenarios.

You can define parameters in your YAML header using the `params` key:

```yaml
---
title: "Regional Sales Report"
params:
  region: "North"
  year: 2026
  show_details: true
---
```

Parameters can be accessed in code through the `params` list:

```{{r}}
#| echo: false
sales_data |>
  filter(region == params$region, year == params$year)
```

```markdown
This report covers the `{{r}} params$region` region for `{{r}} params$year`.
```

It is also possible to override parameters from the command line using the `-P` flag:

```bash
quarto render report.qmd -P region:South -P year:2025
```

For multiple parameter sets, you can create a YAML file with the values:

```{.yaml filename="params-south.yml"}
region: "South"
year: 2025
show_details: false
```

Then reference it when rendering:

```bash
quarto render report.qmd --execute-params params-south.yml
```

### Conditional execution {#sec-conditional-execution}

Sometimes you need code to behave differently depending on the output format.
Quarto provides several ways to access format information from your R code.

The knitr package provides functions to check the output format:

```{{r}}
if (knitr::is_html_output()) {
  # Code for HTML output only
}

if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
  # Code for PDF output only
}
```

You can also check the specific Pandoc output format:

```{{r}}
knitr::pandoc_to()
# Returns e.g., "html", "latex", "docx"
```

You can also use Quarto environment variables for conditional execution.
For more detailed format information, Quarto sets the `QUARTO_EXECUTE_INFO` environment variable during rendering:

```{{r}}
info <- jsonlite::fromJSON(Sys.getenv("QUARTO_EXECUTE_INFO"))
format_name <- info$format$identifier$`base-format`

if (format_name == "html") {
  # HTML-specific code
}
```

This provides finer-grained format details than `knitr::pandoc_to()`, such as sub-format identifiers and format options.

## Rendering {#sec-r-rendering}

### Rendering from R {#sec-render-from-r}

The [**quarto**](https://quarto-dev.github.io/quarto-r/) R package provides functions to render documents directly from R, without using the terminal.
This package is optional â€” you don't need it to use Quarto with R.
It's a convenience for those who prefer working entirely within the R console.

You can use `quarto_render()` to render a document:

```r
library(quarto)

quarto_render("report.qmd")
quarto_render("report.qmd", output_format = "pdf")
```

For iterative work, `quarto_preview()` starts a live preview that updates as you save changes:

```r
quarto_preview("report.qmd")

# When done:
quarto_preview_stop()
```

You can also pass parameters directly when rendering with `quarto_render()` using the `execute_params` argument:

```r
quarto_render(
  "report.qmd",
  execute_params = list(region = "South", year = 2025)
)
```

### Rendering R scripts {#sec-render-scripts}

You can render R scripts (`.R` files) directly with Quarto, without converting them to `.qmd` files first.
This uses syntax based on `knitr::spin()`.

- Lines starting with `#'` are treated as markdown
- Lines starting with `#|` are code cell options
- All other lines are R code

Here's an example script that includes both markdown and code cells:

```{.r filename="analysis.R"}
#' ---
#' title: "Penguin Analysis"
#' author: "Your Name"
#' format: html
#' ---

#' ## Setup

library(palmerpenguins)
library(ggplot2)

#' ## Bill Size Analysis
#'
#' @fig-bill-sizes shows the relationship between
#' bill length and depth.

#| label: fig-bill-sizes
#| fig-cap: "Bill dimensions by species"
#| warning: false

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal()
```

You can render the script just like a `.qmd` file:

```bash
quarto render analysis.R
quarto preview analysis.R
```

This lets you keep your analysis as a plain R script that can also be sourced or run interactively.

## Environment {#sec-r-environment}

### How Quarto finds R {#sec-quarto-finds-r}

Quarto looks for an R installation in the following order:

1. The `QUARTO_R` environment variable, if set
2. The R executable found on your system `PATH`
3. Platform-specific default locations (e.g., `/usr/local/bin/R` on macOS)

If Quarto is using a different R than you expect, you can override the lookup by setting `QUARTO_R` to the path of your preferred R binary.
One way to do this is in a `.env` file in your project directory:

```{.bash filename=".env"}
QUARTO_R=/opt/R/4.4.0/bin/R
```

Quarto automatically reads `.env` files in the project root during rendering.

To see which R installation Quarto is currently using, run `quarto check` from the terminal:

```bash
quarto check knitr
```

The output will include the detected R version and path, along with the knitr and rmarkdown package versions, e.g.:

```bash
Quarto 1.10.3
[âś“] Checking R installation...........OK
      Version: 4.6.0
      Path: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Resources
      LibPaths:
        - /Users/mine/Library/R/arm64/4.6/library
        - /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.6/Resources/library
      knitr: 1.51
      rmarkdown: 2.31

[âś“] Checking Knitr engine render......OK
```

If the R path shown doesn't match what you expect, that's likely the source of any "package not found" or version mismatch issues.

### Diagnosing environment issues {#sec-diagnosing-env}

When Quarto renders a document, it starts a fresh R session.
This session might use a different R installation or library paths than what you use interactively.
In particular, packages you've installed in your interactive session may not be available if the rendering session uses different library paths, and environment variables like `PATH` may also differ.

Add a code cell to your document that outputs your session information:

```{{r}}
#| eval: false
sessionInfo()
.libPaths()
```

This will show the R version and the library paths used during rendering.
For more detailed diagnostics, use the [**sessioninfo**](https://sessioninfo.r-lib.org/) package:

```{{r}}
#| eval: false
sessioninfo::session_info(to_file = "quarto-session-info.txt")
```

Compare either output with your interactive R session to identify discrepancies.
If library paths differ, that explains missing packages; if the R version differs, see @sec-quarto-finds-r for how to control which R installation Quarto uses.

## Multiple languages {#sec-multiple-languages}

While this chapter focuses on R, Quarto documents can include code cells from multiple languages.
Each language runs in its own separate session---objects created in R are not directly accessible from Python, and vice versa.

```{{r}}
x <- 42
```

```{{python}}
# This cannot access the R variable x
y = 100
```

You might, for example, use R for statistical modeling and Python for certain visualizations.

::: callout-tip
## Sharing data between languages

To share data between R and Python cells, save intermediate results to files (e.g., CSV, Parquet) and read them in the other language.
For tighter integration, consider the [**reticulate**](https://rstudio.github.io/reticulate/) package, which allows R and Python to share objects directly.
:::

If your document contains only R code cells, Quarto uses the knitr engine.
If it contains only Python cells, it uses Jupyter.
When both are present, Quarto uses the engine specified in your YAML header, or knitr by default.
